Finite-Sample Crossover in Sensor Classification

Intel Lab Dataset — Real-data validation of the fewer-features phenomenon
Generated 2026-04-14 22:16

Motivation Methodology Modeling Setup Summary Results Comparison Analysis Dataset Report →

Motivation

In classification tasks, adding more features is generally expected to improve accuracy — more information should yield better decisions. However, when training data is limited, the opposite can happen: additional features increase the effective dimensionality of the problem, demanding more samples to estimate decision boundaries reliably. This creates a finite-sample crossover where a lower-dimensional classifier temporarily outperforms a higher-dimensional one.

This phenomenon is well known in asymptotic theory (the “curse of dimensionality”), but its practical manifestation — the specific sample size n* at which the benefit of an extra feature overtakes the cost of estimating it — has received less empirical attention on real sensor data.

This experiment investigates the crossover using real temperature readings from the Intel Berkeley Research Lab. We fix one primary sensor (A) and vary a second sensor (B) across three correlation regimes — high, mid, and low — to answer:

  1. At what sample size n* does the 2-sensor classifier surpass the 1-sensor classifier?
  2. How does the correlation between sensors A and B affect n*?
  3. Can a double crossover occur, where the 2-sensor model is first better, then worse, then better again?

Methodology

We frame the problem as a binary classification task derived from real sensor readings. The pipeline has four stages:

1. Data & Label Construction

From the Intel Lab dataset (54 temperature motes, ~2.3 M readings over 38 days), we build a synchronized time×sensor matrix after removing outliers and filling small gaps (≤ 3 consecutive NaNs). A reference mote R defines binary classes via a median split of its temperature readings: class 0 if temperature ≤ median, class 1 otherwise.

2. Sensor Role Assignment

Sensors are automatically assigned to roles based on pairwise Pearson correlation:

  • A (primary feature) — the mote most correlated with R (ρ ≥ 0.85).
  • Bhigh — high correlation with A (ρBA ≥ 0.95), acting as a near-redundant second feature.
  • Bmid — moderate range (0.5 ≤ ρBR ≤ 0.8), where the utility of the extra sensor is ambiguous.
  • Blow — low correlation (ρBR ≤ 0.4), providing a complementary but noisy feature.

3. Monte Carlo Evaluation

For each scenario (high / mid / low correlation) and dimensionality (d = 1 using sensor A only, or d = 2 using sensors A+B), we draw n training samples uniformly from the pool (500 repetitions per setting). The classifier is evaluated on a fixed stratified hold-out set (20% of the data). We record the mean and standard deviation of classification error across repetitions.

4. Crossover Detection

The crossover point n* is estimated by linear interpolation: we compute Δ(n) = E(d=1) − E(d=2) at each sample size and locate sign changes between consecutive points. Where Δ changes from negative to positive, the 2-sensor model begins to outperform the 1-sensor model, and vice versa.

Modeling

Classifier

We use a linear Support Vector Machine (scikit-learn LinearSVC, C = 1.0, max_iter = 10000). A linear classifier is chosen deliberately: it is sensitive to the dimensionality of the feature space, making the crossover phenomenon visible even at low dimensions (d = 1 vs d = 2). Non-linear kernels or ensemble methods would mask the effect by learning more flexible boundaries that tolerate extra features at small n.

Why not other classifiers?

LDA was considered but removed: its closed-form solution and built-in regularization make it largely insensitive to the 1-vs-2 feature distinction in this dataset. Tree-based methods (Random Forest, Gradient Boosting) perform implicit feature selection, which would also suppress the crossover. The SVM isolates exactly the estimation-cost mechanism we study.

Theoretical model for analysis

In the Analysis section, we fit the error difference to a second-order model:

Δ(n) = G + B/n + C/n²

This quadratic in 1/n captures the asymptotic gap G, the first-order finite-sample penalty B, and a curvature term C that can produce two roots (a double crossover) when its discriminant is positive. The model roots are reported separately from the interpolated n* to highlight the difference between the empirical crossing points and the parametric approximation.

Scenario Comparison

Analysis: Double Crossover

Statistical Significance of Error Differences

For each sample size n and scenario, the paired error difference Δ = E(d=1) − E(d=2) was computed on every Monte Carlo trial (same training subsample, same test set, different feature sets). The figure below shows the mean Δ with its 95% confidence interval. Points where the CI includes zero (non-significant) are marked with an open ring. Wilcoxon signed-rank p-values confirm the results non-parametrically.

Interpretation

The error difference Δ(n) = E(d=1) − E(d=2) captures the net benefit of adding a second sensor at each sample size. Positive Δ means d=2 outperforms d=1; negative means d=1 is better alone. The three scenarios produce qualitatively different trajectories because of how the bias–variance tradeoff interacts with the correlation structure of sensor B.

Regime 1 — Small n: variance dominates

With very few training samples, estimating a decision boundary in 2-D requires fitting twice as many effective parameters as in 1-D. The additional estimation variance overwhelms whatever signal sensor B provides, so d=1 tends to match or beat d=2 at the smallest sample sizes. This effect is visible in all three scenarios at n = 2 and is strongest where B is least informative (high-correlation, where B is nearly redundant with A).

Regime 2 — Moderate n: signal emerges

As n grows, the variance cost of the extra dimension falls as O(1/n). Once enough samples are available to reliably estimate the 2-D boundary, the additional label-relevant information in B begins to help. In the low-correlation scenario, B is largely independent of A, so the benefit is large and persistent. In the mid-correlation scenario, the benefit is real but modest, because B’s signal partially overlaps with A’s.

Regime 3 — Large n: asymptotic bias

When n is large enough that variance is negligible, the irreducible error of the model class determines performance. A linear SVM draws a hyperplane; in 1-D this is a simple threshold on sensor A, which is highly correlated with the reference sensor R. Adding sensor B forces the hyperplane to operate in 2-D, where the optimal linear boundary must compromise between A’s strong signal and B’s weaker or partially redundant signal.

In the mid-correlation scenario, this compromise produces a slightly worse asymptotic boundary than the 1-D threshold. Consequently Δ turns negative at large n, creating the second crossover. For low-correlation, B contributes enough independent information that the 2-D boundary remains better than 1-D even asymptotically — only a single crossover occurs. For high-correlation, B is so similar to A that the asymptotic boundary is barely affected and the only crossover is due to the small-n variance effect.

Conditions for a Double Crossover

A double crossover requires two ingredients:

  1. Variance-induced advantage for d=1 at small n — always present because estimation variance scales with dimensionality.
  2. Asymptotic bias favouring d=1 at large n — occurs when the linear SVM’s optimal 2-D hyperplane is a worse approximation of the true decision boundary than its 1-D threshold. This happens when sensor B is moderately correlated with both A and R: informative enough to pull the boundary off its optimal 1-D direction, but not informative enough to improve it.

When both conditions hold, Δ(n) starts negative (d=1 better), turns positive in the middle range (d=2 better), and returns to negative (d=1 better again). The mid-correlation scenario is the only one where B occupies this “ambiguous utility” zone.

Parametric Model (Reference)

As a compact summary, the mean Δ(n) can be approximated by Δ(n) = G + B/n + C/n². Two real positive roots of this quadratic in 1/n correspond to two crossover points. The fitted coefficients are shown below. Note: the interpolated n* values reported elsewhere in this report are more accurate since they do not assume this parametric form.

ScenarioGBC n* (model)R²
high-correlation +0.00204-0.08772+0.064 42.3 0.904
mid-correlation -0.00218+0.07652-0.207 3.0, 32.1 0.763
low-correlation +0.01516-0.20391+0.131 12.8 0.996
